forward.finding: CLI resolves paths relative to CWD, not project_root. If user runs from a subdirectory, target paths may be outside project_root.

No validation that language strings from CLI match registered fixers. If --lang is 'all', no language is detected via CLI args, so fallthrough to auto-detect works — but if someone passes --lang='unknown', it silently skips.
Error handling in _collect_fix_actions uses getattr/setattr pattern to capture errors into _collect_errors, but this is reset each call — errors from previous iterations are lost.

The convergence loop calls _collect_fix_actions() each iteration. But after applying fixes in iteration N, the files on disk have changed. _verify_all() checks if issues remain, but _collect_fix_actions() re-reads from disk. This is correct, BUT the verify() method on most fixers returns an empty list (stub), so convergence detection is incomplete.

Base verify() returns empty list. Only the orchestration engine calls verify(). Most concrete fixers do NOT override verify(), so the convergence loop relies entirely on whether analyze() returns actions on subsequent iterations. If analyze() is idempotent (returns same actions each time), the loop will infinite-iterate. There's no fixpoint detection.

The Ruff fixer runs `ruff check --fix` on ALL files at once (batch mode), then iterates diagnostics. But it also runs `ruff format` per-file. If ruff check --fix modifies a file, the subsequent ruff format reads the original (pre-fix) content because original was captured before the batch command. This means the format might be stale.

Runs `ruff check --fix` which MODIFIES files on disk as a side effect, then also captures stdout JSON. But the fix result comparison `fix_result.stdout != original` via stdin is a SEPARATE command. So we're running ruff check --fix twice: once with file args (modifies disk), once with stdin (piped). First run already modified files.
ESLint --fix also modifies files in-place (side effect), then runs Prettier via stdin. But the ESLint command writes changes to disk before we capture the diff.

golangci-lint --fix modifies files on disk. Then re-reads file content for comparison. But the 'original' was captured BEFORE the tool ran, so if the disk file changed by the batch command, original is correct but we've already applied the fix. The FixAction then gets written back via apply_fix() which writes fixed_content over the already-fixed file.

reverse.req: --check mode: exit code 1 if fixes needed
reverse.finding: --check mode: exit code 1 if fixes needed: Partially — works but verify() stubbed
Backup original files before applying: NOT IMPLEMENTED
Parallel execution across files: NOT IMPLEMENTED — sequential per-file
Incremental mode (only changed files): NOT IMPLEMENTED
Plugin system for custom fixers: NOT IMPLEMENTED
Dry run with detailed report: Partially — --diff shows per-action

adversarial.finding: file_paths are converted to strings for CLI commands via str(file_path). If a file is named like ';rm -rf /;.py' or has spaces, the subprocess may interpret it unexpectedly. Python's subprocess.run with list args is safe against shell injection, but paths with special chars may still cause tool failures.
No content validation before writing fixed content to disk. If a tool returns corrupted content (e.g., null bytes, encoding issues), write_text will write it as-is. Could corrupt source files.
detect() methods use rglob() which follows symlinks by default. A symlink pointing to /etc/passwd or similar could be read and written to.
No file locking. If two FixEngine instances run on the same files concurrently, they'll corrupt each other's changes.
No cleanup of temporary files if subprocess fails mid-way.
No size limits on file reads. A multi-GB file will OOM the process.
No normalization of file paths or content. Homograph attacks could bypass file detection.
If a tool (ruff, eslint, goimports) is not installed, the fixer silently skips (only prints if verbose). User may think fixes were applied when they weren't.

edge_cases.finding: If target_paths is an empty directory, all fixers return empty file lists. Pipeline reports 'No fixes needed' — correct behavior.
If directory exists but no supported file types, _collect_fix_actions returns empty. Loop exits iteration 0. FixResult has 0 actions. Correct.
detect() methods only check file extension, not content. Binary files with .py extension (pyc, pyo, so) would be passed to ruff which will fail.
No file size check. read_text() on a 100MB+ file will consume significant memory. On 4GB workstation, this could trigger OOM.
apply_fix() catches Exception and returns False silently. Read-only files will be skipped without warning (unless verbose).
All read_text/write_text use encoding='utf-8' explicitly. Non-UTF8 files (e.g., Latin-1, shift-JIS) will throw UnicodeDecodeError.
_run_command has no timeout. If a tool hangs (e.g., clang-tidy on a large file), the fixer blocks indefinitely.
Empty file → read_text returns ''. Comparison with ruff/stdin output 'original != fixed' — if both empty, no action. Correct.
In check/diff mode, the pipeline breaks after the FIRST iteration regardless of whether more fixes would be needed. This gives incomplete diffs.
If two fixers fight each other (e.g., eslint changes → prettier changes back → eslint changes → ...), the loop runs all max_iterations. No oscillation detection.
